skillName: Understand angle notation and label angles correctly
misconceptionSummary: Students do not realise the middle letter of ∠ABC is the vertex of the angle.

visuals.ruleText: In ∠ABC, the middle letter B is the vertex — the point where the arms meet
visuals.subText: A and C are points on the two arms — they define which angle at B is meant

steps.narration: The angle symbol followed by three letters tells you exactly which angle is meant. The middle letter always names the vertex — the corner of the angle. The first and last letters name points on each arm, identifying the two sides of the angle.

lines.text: Arms go from Q toward P and from Q toward R
The angle is at Q, between arm QP and arm QR

steps.narration: In angle PQR, the vertex is Q — the middle letter. One arm runs from Q toward P and the other from Q toward R. The angle is the opening at Q between those two arms.

lines.text: To label angle at B in triangle ABC:
Write ∠ABC or ∠CBA — B is in the middle
∠BAC would label the angle at A instead  ✗

steps.narration: In triangle ABC, to label the angle at vertex B, write angle ABC or angle CBA — B must be the middle letter. Writing angle BAC would label the angle at A instead, because A would then be in the middle.

visuals.expression: ∠ABC → vertex at B   ∠PQR → vertex at Q
visuals.label: Middle letter = vertex — always

steps.narration: Angle ABC has its vertex at B. Angle PQR has its vertex at Q. The middle letter always names the vertex — that is the one rule to remember about angle notation.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: thinking the first letter of ∠ABC names the vertex. The vertex is always the middle letter — B in ∠ABC. The outer letters A and C are on the arms.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: In angle notation, the middle letter is the vertex — the corner of the angle. The first and last letters are points on the two arms. ∠ABC has its angle at B, not at A.
